chunk_summary: National Quality Assurance Standards (NQAS) for milk bank services ensure traceability, safety controls, and continuous quality improvement in donor human milk (DHM) processing, storage, and dispensing.
learning_objectives: Understand the traceability requirements for donor human milk (DHM) under NQAS.
Learn the quarantine-and-release protocol for pasteurized DHM and microbiological safety measures.
Identify key indicators for continuous quality improvement in human milk banking.
Explore corrective actions for deviations in milk bank processes.
key_facts: Traceability links each DHM bottle to donor screening, pasteurization batch, time-temperature records, culture results, and dispensing logs.
Pasteurized DHM must be quarantined until microbiology confirms acceptable culture results; culture-positive batches are discarded.
Temperature assurance includes routine refrigerator/freezer monitoring, defined responses to excursions, and backup power planning.
Indicators for quality improvement include culture-failure rate, discard rate due to cold-chain breach, and wrong-milk near-miss frequency.
Corrective actions for deviations include stock quarantine/discard, environmental cleaning, staff retraining, and equipment servicing.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: The core expectation is complete traceability: each bottle of released DHM is linked to donor screening records, a pasteurization batch identifier, the pasteurizer time–temperature record, post-pasteurization culture results, and the recipient dispensing log.
document_key_topics: Traceability in donor human milk processing
Continuous quality improvement in milk banking
